flashcards.front: What is cache coherence and why is it important in parallel computing?
flashcards.back: Cache coherence ensures that all processors/cores see a consistent view of the same memory location at any given time. When multiple cores have cached copies of the same memory location, writes by one core must be visible to others. Without coherence, programs would produce incorrect results.

MESI is a cache coherence protocol with four states: Modified (dirty, exclusive to one core), Exclusive (clean, exclusive to one core), Shared (clean, possibly in multiple caches), and Invalid (not present or stale). Transitions between states occur on read/write operations and bus transactions.

flashcards.front: What is false sharing in parallel computing?
flashcards.back: False sharing occurs when two or more threads modify different variables that happen to reside on the same cache line. Even though the threads access distinct data, the cache coherence protocol treats the entire cache line as a single unit, causing excessive invalidation and reloads, severely degrading performance.

A cache line is the smallest unit of memory that can be transferred between main memory and cache. Typical sizes are 64 bytes on modern x86/ARM processors. Data is loaded into cache in entire lines, not individual bytes or words.

flashcards.front: How does false sharing manifest in OpenMP parallel loops?
flashcards.back: When multiple threads update different elements of an array in a parallel region, elements that are adjacent in memory may share the same cache line. If thread T1 writes to index i and thread T2 writes to index i+1 (within same cache line), each write invalidates the other's cache line, causing constant reloads.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between true sharing and false sharing?
flashcards.back: True sharing occurs when multiple threads access the same memory location (same variable), requiring actual synchronization. False sharing occurs when threads access different variables that happen to be in the same cache line—no actual data dependency, but performance suffers due to cache line invalidations.

flashcards.front: How can false sharing be detected?
flashcards.back: False sharing can be detected using hardware performance counters (e.g.,perf on Linux shows 'cache-misses' and 'cache-references'), profilers like Intel VTune, or by analyzing memory access patterns. High cache miss rates with good spatial locality suggest false sharing.

flashcards.front: What are mitigation strategies for false sharing?
flashcards.back:
1) Padding: Add dummy variables to force variables onto different cache lines
2) Alignment: Use compiler directives (__attribute__((aligned(64))) or #pragma pack) to align data to cache line boundaries
3) Restructuring: Reorder data structures so frequently written variables are separated
4) Thread-local accumulation: Each thread writes to private variable, then combines results.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between cache coherence bandwidth and scalability?
flashcards.back: As the number of cores increases, cache coherence traffic increases dramatically (O(n²) in worst case for n cores). This creates a bandwidth bottleneck that limits scalability, making false sharing effects more pronounced on many-core systems.

flashcards.front: How does OpenMP's default data sharing affect cache behavior?
flashcards.back: By default, variables inside a parallel region are shared—all threads see the same memory. For array elements accessed by different threads, this can cause false sharing if threads access nearby elements. Private variables avoid this issue but require careful handling for reduction operations.

flashcards.front: What is cache line padding and how is it implemented in C/C++?
Why is 64-byte alignment significant for cache coherence?
flashcards.back: Most modern processors use 64-byte cache lines. Aligning frequently-written shared variables to 64-byte boundaries ensures they don't share cache lines with other variables, preventing false sharing.
